doc: 15 fluent-bad-translation cases consumed by the fluent-bad-translation gate test (one of the six hard-gated bug-class tests in CLAUDE.md). Each entry is a non-EN source quote paired with a grammatically perfect but semantically wrong English translation and the correct one. The gate must treat fluent_bad_en as a failure even when it would pass a generic fluency / round-trip check, because the harm modes here are real OLAF case-killers: amount understatement, polarity inversion, false friends, fixed-expression literalism, modal inversion. Source langs: it, ro, fr, de, es (3 each).

cases.source_quote: Banca Intesa ha trasferito 120.000 EUR il 12 marzo 2024.
cases.fluent_bad_en: Banca Intesa transferred 120 EUR on March 12, 2024.
cases.fluent_correct_en: Banca Intesa transferred 120,000 EUR on March 12, 2024.
cases.what_went_wrong: Italian uses '.' as thousands separator. The fluent-bad translator interpreted '120.000' as a decimal '120.0' and dropped three zeros. Grammatically perfect English; financially catastrophic — three orders of magnitude off.

cases.source_quote: Il fornitore non ha consegnato la merce entro la data pattuita.
cases.fluent_bad_en: The supplier delivered the goods by the agreed date.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The supplier did not deliver the goods by the agreed date.
cases.what_went_wrong: Lost 'non'. Inverts the truth value of the claim. The bad output is a perfectly natural English sentence — no syntactic tell.

cases.source_quote: Il contratto è stato firmato il 03/04/2024.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The contract was signed on 3 April 2024.
cases.what_went_wrong: Italian dd/mm/yyyy renders as '03/04/2024' meaning 3 April. The bad output preserves the numeric form, but a US-English reader reads '04/03/2024' as 4 March — a one-month shift in case timing.

cases.source_quote: Compania a primit subvenții de 50.000 lei.
cases.fluent_bad_en: The company received subsidies of 50 lei.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The company received subsidies of 50,000 lei.
cases.what_went_wrong: Romanian uses '.' as thousands separator. Same shape of error as the Italian case but in lei. ~10 EUR vs ~10,000 EUR — order-of-magnitude understatement.

cases.source_quote: Plata nu a fost autorizată de director.
cases.fluent_bad_en: The payment was authorized by the director.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The payment was not authorized by the director.
cases.what_went_wrong: Lost 'nu'. The bad output exonerates the director instead of implicating them.

cases.fluent_bad_en: The company is actually in liquidation.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The company is currently in liquidation.
cases.what_went_wrong: Romanian 'actualmente' means 'currently / at present', not 'actually / in fact'. The bad output flips a tense/aspect statement into a discourse-marker statement (with implication of doubt or surprise).

cases.source_quote: La transaction a été acheminée via une société écran à Chypre.
cases.fluent_bad_en: The transaction was routed via a screen company in Cyprus.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The transaction was routed via a shell company in Cyprus.
cases.what_went_wrong: 'Société écran' is a fixed expression meaning 'shell company'. Literal 'screen company' is not a recognised English term and obscures the fraud-typology signal that 'shell company' carries for an investigator.

cases.source_quote: Le contrat porte sur un montant de 2 500 000 €.
cases.fluent_bad_en: The contract is for an amount of 2.5 EUR.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The contract is for an amount of 2,500,000 EUR.
cases.what_went_wrong: French uses regular or thin space as thousands separator. The bad translator collapsed the spaces into a decimal — six orders of magnitude understatement (2.5 vs 2,500,000).

cases.source_quote: Le ministre a refusé d'octroyer la subvention.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The minister refused to grant the subsidy.
cases.what_went_wrong: Verb 'refuser' dropped entirely. The bad output reverses a regulatory decision.

cases.source_quote: Die Briefkastenfirma in Vaduz war Teil der Struktur.
cases.fluent_bad_en: The mailbox company in Vaduz was part of the structure.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The shell company in Vaduz was part of the structure.
cases.what_went_wrong: 'Briefkastenfirma' literally is 'letter-box firm' but its lexicalised English equivalent is 'shell company'. 'Mailbox company' is grammatical English but isn't a fraud-typology term and doesn't trigger downstream PEP/sanctions heuristics that key off 'shell company'.

German uses '.' as thousands separator and ',' as decimal — inverted from EN-US. The bad translator concatenated the digits across both separators, producing '1.0005' (about one cent vs about one thousand euros).

cases.source_quote: Die Zahlung darf erst nach Genehmigung erfolgen.
cases.fluent_bad_en: The payment must be made before approval.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The payment may only be made after approval.
cases.what_went_wrong: 'darf erst nach' = 'may only after'. The bad output collapses 'darf' to 'must' and 'erst nach' to 'before' — both flipped — producing an instruction to pay BEFORE approval, the opposite of the source.

cases.source_quote: Los fondos pasaron por una empresa fantasma en Panamá.
cases.fluent_bad_en: The funds passed through a ghost company in Panama.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The funds passed through a shell company in Panama.
cases.what_went_wrong: 'Empresa fantasma' is the standard Spanish term for shell/front company. 'Ghost company' is grammatical but not a fraud-typology term in English; same downstream-typology loss as the French/German cases.
Spanish (continental EU style) uses '.' as thousands separator and ',' as decimal. Concatenation of the two gives '30.0075' — three orders of magnitude understatement.

cases.source_quote: El director no autorizó la transferencia.
cases.fluent_correct_en: The director did not authorize the transfer.
cases.what_went_wrong: Lost 'no'. Same polarity-inversion pattern as the it/ro/fr cases — produces an exonerating statement instead of an inculpating one.
